title: Switching to an off-peak electricity tariff
subtitle: Which appliances to shift, what savings to expect, and how time-of-use tariffs actually work
excerpt: Time-of-use electricity tariffs charge different rates at different hours. Octopus Agile can drop below 5p/kWh overnight; the same unit costs 27p in the evening peak. Shifting dishwasher, washing machine, tumble dryer, and EV charging to off-peak hours typically saves a modest cost in a medium-sized household.
sourceNotes: Ofgem electricity tariff structures and consumer guidance. Octopus Energy tariff design documentation for Agile and Go. BEIS National Grid ESO demand-side flexibility programme. Energy Saving Trust guide to smart electricity tariffs. Gov.uk smart meter rollout programme for half-hourly settlement requirement.

glossaryTerms.definition: The carbon dioxide equivalent emitted per kWh of electricity generated by the national grid, measured in gCO2eq/kWh. It is lowest overnight when wind generation is high and lowest in summer.

content.text: Time-of-use tariffs charge more at peak demand (typically 4-8 pm on weekdays) and less overnight. Octopus Agile follows the wholesale spot price: some overnight slots fall below 5p/kWh. Economy 7 is simpler: 7 cheap hours overnight at around 12p/kWh, standard rate the rest of the day.
Dishwasher: a full-load wash at 27p/kWh (1.2 kWh) costs 32p. At 12p/kWh it costs 14p. Running one wash per day shifted overnight: saving of a modest cost.
Washing machine: a 40°C cotton wash uses around 0.7 kWh. Shifted to overnight from evening peak: saving around a modest cost per daily wash load.
Tumble dryer: 3-4 kWh per cycle. Highest consumption appliance for shift savings. One cycle per day: saving of a modest cost shifted from 27p to 12p.

EV charging: the biggest opportunity for most EV owners. A 60 kWh battery from 20% to 100% = 48 kWh. At 27p that is a modest cost; at 7p overnight (Octopus Go or similar) it is a modest cost.36. Annual saving for 12,000 miles/year EV driving: a modest cost.
Hot water immersion heater: if you heat water electrically, shift the immersion heater to a timer on the overnight rate. 3 kWh/day heated at 12p instead of 27p: saving of a modest cost.
A smart meter (required by most time-of-use tariffs for half-hourly metering). If you do not have one, contact your supplier.
Appliances with delay-start functions (most modern dishwashers, washing machines, and dryers have this).
A smart plug timer for appliances without built-in delay (immersion heater, older dishwasher).
Economy 7 (legacy, simple): 7 fixed cheap hours overnight. Good for storage heaters, immersion heaters, EV charging on a consistent overnight schedule.
Octopus Go: flat cheap rate of around 7p/kWh for 4 hours at 12-5 am, standard rate otherwise. Simple to understand, good for EV charging.
Octopus Agile: half-hourly price follows the spot market. Lowest possible cost but requires active management (an Octopus app or home automation system to shift loads to cheap slots). Grid
is also lowest on overnight renewable-heavy slots. Agile is often the greenest tariff as well as the cheapest.
